Major depressive disorder, single episode at age 22, in remission
Irritable bowel syndrome, diagnosed at age 20
Childhood history of sexual abuse, disclosed during prior psychiatric treatment

Maternal aunt has epilepsy (generalized tonic-clonic seizures, well-controlled on levetiracetam)
No family history of multiple sclerosis or stroke

social_history.drugs: Denies illicit drug use, denies marijuana
social_history.occupation: Elementary school teacher, currently on medical leave for 3 weeks due to symptoms
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her boyfriend in an apartment; reports recent relationship conflict
social_history.exercise: Previously active, ran 3 miles three times per week, has stopped due to leg weakness
social_history.psychosocial: Reports significant work stress related to a difficult classroom assignment; recently learned of a close friend's cancer diagnosis

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Affect is anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria. Attention intact, able to recite months of the year backward. Memory 3/3 immediate and delayed recall. No aphasia. Fund of knowledge intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam normal, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus or diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all extremities, no spasticity or rigidity. Right lower extremity: give-way weakness pattern noted in hip flexion, knee extension, and ankle dorsiflexion — initial resistance to full strength followed by sudden collapse of effort. Formal testing yields 2-3/5 in these muscle groups with inconsistency on repeat testing. Left lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally. Hoover sign POSITIVE on the right — involuntary hip extension on the right is strong when testing left hip flexion against resistance, but voluntary right hip flexion appears weak. No pronator drift in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Patient reports decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the entire right leg extending to the level of the inguinal crease, with a sharp midline demarcation that splits the trunk exactly at the midline. Vibration and proprioception are reported as diminished in the right toes, but testing with distraction shows intact proprioception. Left leg sensation intact throughout. Upper extremity sensation intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally (including right side despite reported weakness), Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing normal on the left; patient reports inability to perform on the right due to weakness, but when distracted the right leg moves smoothly to the shin. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact in upper extremities.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient walks with a dragging right leg pattern. The gait is effortful-appearing but non-physiological — the leg drags behind with the hip externally rotated but knee does not buckle. When distracted during conversation while ambulating, leg function intermittently improves. No circumduction. Romberg testing: marked swaying with eyes closed but catches herself repeatedly without falling (positive for functional sway). Tandem gait not attempted by patient due to reported weakness.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a paroxysmal episode of bilateral upper extremity shaking lasting approximately 90 seconds. Eyes were tightly closed with resistance to opening, and she appeared to retain awareness (responded to verbal commands during the event). The shaking was irregular, variable in amplitude, and waxed and waned. No tonic phase, no cyanosis, no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence. She was immediately alert and conversant after the episode with no post-ictal confusion. This was clinically consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES).

patient.chief_complaint: Right leg weakness and episodes of shaking spells over the past 3 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Lucia Reyes is a 26-year-old right-handed woman and elementary school teacher who presents as an inpatient transfer from the emergency department after three weeks of progressive right leg weakness and recurrent episodes of uncontrollable shaking. The symptoms began abruptly three weeks ago when she awoke one morning and found that her right leg felt heavy and difficult to move. She reports that she was unable to bear weight on the right leg and fell twice that first day. Over the following days, the weakness persisted and she began using a cane provided by a friend. Approximately one week after the onset of weakness, she experienced her first shaking episode while at home: her arms and legs began trembling, she fell to the floor, and the episode lasted roughly two minutes. Her boyfriend witnessed the event and reports that she appeared awake during the episode and could respond to her name, though she later described feeling detached from her body. She has since had five additional similar episodes, occurring roughly every two to three days, sometimes triggered by emotional stress or arguments. Emergency medical services were called during the third episode and she was brought to the emergency department, where a CT head was normal and she was discharged with neurology follow-up. She returned to the ED two days ago after two episodes occurred in the same day, one at home and one in the waiting room of her primary care physician's office. The waiting room episode was witnessed by nursing staff, who noted bilateral arm shaking with eyes closed and no loss of continence. She was admitted for further workup. She denies headache, visual changes, numbness or tingling in the upper extremities, bowel or bladder incontinence, fever, weight loss, or recent illness. She reports significant emotional distress related to work stress, relationship difficulties, and learning of a close friend's cancer diagnosis shortly before symptom onset. She was previously seen by a psychiatrist for anxiety and depression but has not had regular follow-up in the past year.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-moderate amplitude beta activity, symmetric, likely related to anxious state or sertraline use
background.sleep_features: Brief stage 2 sleep captured with vertex waves and sleep spindles, normal architecture

findings.clinical_correlation: During recording, the patient had a clinical event consisting of bilateral upper extremity tremor and whole-body shaking lasting 75 seconds. The EEG showed NO ictal correlate — the background remained a normal awake alpha rhythm throughout the event with superimposed muscle and movement artifact. No epileptiform discharges, no rhythmic theta or delta slowing, no post-event slowing. This is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES).

artifacts.description: During clinical event, obscures some channels but underlying rhythms discernible on referential montage
Intermittent, correlates with patient anxiety, does not obscure posterior dominant rhythm

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Normal buildup of diffuse slowing with prompt recovery. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Patient reported feeling lightheaded but no clinical event occurred.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at standard flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. One clinical event was captured during the recording consisting of generalized shaking lasting 75 seconds with NO electrographic correlate. The normal EEG background throughout the event is diagnostic for a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). No interictal epileptiform abnormalities were identified. No seizures were captured.
eeg.limitations: Single 60-minute routine video-EEG recording. While one event was captured with no EEG correlate, prolonged monitoring may be considered if additional characterization is desired.
recommended_actions: Communicate PNES diagnosis to patient and treatment team with sensitivity
Referral to psychiatry and clinical psychology for cognitive behavioral therapy
Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring only if diagnostic uncertainty remains

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI/ADC sequences
No white matter signal abnormality to suggest demyelinating disease
Normal ventricular system size and configuration
No mass lesion or space-occupying process
Normal brain parenchymal volume for age
Normal flow voids in major intracranial vessels
No restricted diffusion in the corticospinal tracts
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast. No evidence of stroke, demyelinating disease, mass lesion, or structural abnormality to account for the patient's right leg weakness or paroxysmal events. The normal MRI in the context of positive functional neurological signs on examination supports a diagnosis of functional neurological disorder.
recommended_actions: No further neuroimaging indicated based on current findings
Clinical correlation with positive functional examination signs is recommended

Inflammatory_markers.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of systemic inflammatory process

Post_event_prolactin.clinical_significance: Normal post-event prolactin. In epileptic generalized tonic-clonic or complex partial seizures, prolactin is typically elevated 2-3x baseline within 10-20 minutes. The normal level supports a non-epileptic etiology for the clinical event.

Autoimmune_screen.clinical_significance: No evidence of systemic autoimmune disease

labs.interpretation: All laboratory studies are within normal limits. CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, ANA, vitamin B12, and folate are all normal. Post-event prolactin level drawn 25 minutes after a witnessed clinical event is not elevated, which argues against an epileptic seizure as the etiology. There is no laboratory evidence of metabolic, endocrine, autoimmune, or inflammatory disease to account for the patient's symptoms.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive, well-organized throughout 48-hour recording
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with appropriate cycling through stages. Vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes identified. No abnormalities in sleep.

findings.clinical_correlation: Episode at 14:23 on day 1: bilateral arm and leg shaking, eyes closed, duration 110 seconds. Patient responsive to voice during event. NO electrographic correlate — normal alpha rhythm maintained throughout. Consistent with PNES.

Episode at 09:47 on day 2: right leg stiffening followed by whole-body tremor, duration 85 seconds, occurred immediately after a phone conversation. Patient eyes tightly shut, resisted passive eye opening. NO electrographic correlate. Consistent with PNES.

Episode at 16:10 on day 2: bilateral upper extremity shaking with pelvic thrusting, duration 65 seconds. Patient appeared to be aware, immediately conversant afterward. NO electrographic correlate. Consistent with PNES.

artifacts.description: During all three clinical events, significant movement and muscle artifact obscures some channels. However, on referential montage with posterior electrodes, the underlying background rhythm is clearly maintained without ictal patterns.

output.impression: Prolonged 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring captured three habitual clinical events, all demonstrating NO electrographic correlate on EEG. The background remained normal throughout all events. This is diagnostic for psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). No interictal epileptiform abnormalities were identified at any point during the 48-hour recording. No electrographic seizures occurred. This study confirms the diagnosis of functional neurological disorder with non-epileptic seizures.
output.limitations: None significant. Three habitual events captured with adequate EEG quality to confirm absence of ictal correlate.
recommended_actions: Definitive diagnosis of PNES — communicate to patient using recommended frameworks
Referral to psychotherapy, specifically CBT for PNES
Avoid antiepileptic drug initiation or escalation
Psychiatric evaluation for comorbid anxiety and trauma history

Right peroneal motor nerve - conduction velocity
Right tibial motor nerve - distal latency
Right tibial motor nerve - conduction velocity

Normal insertional activity, no denervation potentials, normal MUP morphology, full recruitment
Normal insertional activity, no fibrillations, normal MUP morphology, full recruitment
No fibrillations or positive sharp waves, normal

output.interpretation: Electrodiagnostic study (EMG/NCS) of the right lower extremity is entirely normal. Motor and sensory nerve conduction studies show normal latencies, amplitudes, and conduction velocities. Needle EMG of right lower extremity muscles and lumbar paraspinals shows no evidence of denervation, myopathy, or neuropathy. This rules out peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, plexopathy, and myopathy as causes of the patient's right leg weakness. The normal electrodiagnostic findings in the context of give-way weakness and a positive Hoover sign support the diagnosis of functional neurological disorder.

additional_observations: Cervical spine: Normal alignment, no disc herniation, no spinal cord signal abnormality, no cord compression
Thoracic spine: Normal alignment, normal cord caliber and signal throughout, no enhancing lesions
Lumbar spine: Normal alignment, no significant disc herniation or canal stenosis, conus medullaris terminates at L1 level (normal), no intradural abnormality
No evidence of demyelinating plaques in the spinal cord
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine with and without gadolinium contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating disease, cord compression, radiculopathy, or structural abnormality. The normal spinal MRI effectively rules out myelopathy and structural spinal cord pathology as a cause of the patient's lower extremity weakness.
recommended_actions: Clinical findings are most consistent with functional neurological disorder

Cognitive_screening.clinical_significance: Normal, lost points on delayed recall likely related to anxiety and inattention

Psychological_measures.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe

Psychological_measures.clinical_significance: Score above cutoff suggestive of PTSD; patient endorsed re-experiencing, avoidance, and hyperarousal symptoms related to childhood trauma
Effort and engagement with testing were adequate; no evidence of malingering or feigned cognitive impairment

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological evaluation reveals normal cognitive function with no evidence of organic cognitive impairment. Psychological screening demonstrates moderate depression (PHQ-9 = 14), severe anxiety (GAD-7 = 16), and a positive PTSD screen (PCL-5 = 38) with symptoms related to a disclosed history of childhood sexual abuse. Symptom validity testing was passed, indicating genuine engagement. The psychiatric comorbidity profile is highly consistent with functional neurological disorder and supports the formulation that psychological factors are relevant to the onset and maintenance of symptoms.

Psychiatric_assessment.value: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness and non-epileptic seizures (F44.4)
Psychiatric_assessment.clinical_significance: Primary neurological diagnosis, confirmed by neurology with positive clinical signs
Related to childhood sexual abuse; likely predisposing factor for FND
Recurrent episode in context of current stressors

Psychiatric_assessment.value: Low — no suicidal ideation, no plan, no intent, no prior attempts
No evidence of malingering or factitious disorder; symptom presentation consistent with genuine functional neurological disorder
Psychiatric_assessment.clinical_significance: Important to distinguish FND from malingering — FND symptoms are involuntary

output.interpretation: Formal psychiatric evaluation confirms comorbid generalized anxiety disorder, PTSD related to childhood trauma, and recurrent major depressive disorder. These psychiatric conditions are recognized predisposing and perpetuating factors for functional neurological disorder. The temporal relationship between recent psychosocial stressors (work stress, relationship conflict, friend's illness) and symptom onset is notable. No evidence of malingering or factitious disorder. Recommended treatment includes trauma-focused psychotherapy (EMDR or prolonged exposure), CBT specifically adapted for FND and PNES, optimization of antidepressant medication (consider dose increase of sertraline to 150-200 mg), and multidisciplinary rehabilitation with physiotherapy using a functional retraining approach.
abnormal_values_summary: Comorbid GAD, PTSD, and recurrent MDD identified
PTSD linked to childhood sexual abuse
Psychiatric comorbidity profile consistent with FND

output.query: functional neurological disorder psychogenic non-epileptic seizures treatment evidence

results.title: Cognitive behavioral therapy for functional neurological disorder: A randomized controlled trial (CODES trial)
results.key_findings: CBT specifically adapted for PNES reduced seizure frequency compared to standard medical care

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.key_findings: FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs (e.g., Hoover sign, entrainment) rather than by exclusion; integrated biopsychosocial models explain pathophysiology

results.title: Physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms: a consensus recommendation
results.key_findings: Specialized physiotherapy using motor retraining and distraction techniques shows benefit for FND motor symptoms

output.summary: Current evidence supports a multidisciplinary treatment approach for FND including: (1) CBT specifically adapted for functional neurological symptoms/PNES (CODES trial evidence), (2) specialized physiotherapy using motor retraining techniques, (3) treatment of psychiatric comorbidities including anxiety, depression, and PTSD, and (4) clear, empathic communication of the diagnosis as a positive diagnosis made by clinical signs rather than a diagnosis of exclusion. Antiepileptic drugs have no role in PNES treatment.

output.proposed: Sertraline 150 mg daily (dose increase from 100 mg)
warnings: Monitor for activation syndrome (increased anxiety, agitation) with dose increase, especially in first 1-2 weeks
Avoid abrupt discontinuation — taper if switching medications
alternatives: Venlafaxine 75-150 mg daily (SNRI, may benefit comorbid pain symptoms)
Duloxetine 60 mg daily (SNRI, may help with comorbid IBS symptoms)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with motor symptoms (right leg weakness) and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures

differential.key_distinguishing: EEG captured clinical events with NO electrographic correlate, which is diagnostic for PNES. Retained awareness during events, eyes tightly closed (ictal eye closure is rare in epileptic seizures), irregular variable-amplitude shaking, no post-ictal confusion, normal post-event prolactin.
Normal brain and spine MRI with no white matter lesions. Normal inflammatory markers. No optic neuritis or internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Age and sex demographics overlap, but imaging rules this out.

Patient is 26 years old with no vascular risk factors. Normal MRI with no DWI restriction. Weakness does not follow a vascular territory. Give-way weakness and positive Hoover sign are inconsistent with upper motor neuron lesion.

Normal EMG/NCS rules out peripheral nerve pathology. Preserved reflexes in the weak limb are inconsistent with lower motor neuron lesion. Sensory loss has non-anatomic distribution (midline splitting).
No fatigable weakness, no ptosis, no diplopia, no bulbar symptoms. Give-way pattern is not consistent with myasthenia. Normal EMG with no decremental response.

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination specifically looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, entrainment, distractibility)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistent exam findings with distraction, non-anatomic sensory loss

optimal_actions.action: Order routine video-EEG to capture a habitual event and assess for electrographic correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical event captured with NO EEG correlate, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to rule out structural lesion
Obtain basic laboratory studies (CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers) and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal including post-event prolactin, supporting non-epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Communicate the diagnosis of FND/PNES to the patient using an empathic, transparent approach emphasizing positive diagnostic signs
Refer to psychiatry for evaluation and management of comorbid anxiety, depression, and PTSD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Comorbid GAD, PTSD, recurrent MDD identified

optimal_actions.action: Refer to clinical psychology for cognitive behavioral therapy adapted for FND/PNES
Refer to physiotherapy for specialized functional motor symptom rehabilitation using retraining and distraction techniques
Consider optimization of antidepressant medication (increase sertraline or switch to SNRI)
Arrange outpatient multidisciplinary follow-up with neurology, psychiatry, psychology, and physiotherapy

critical_actions: Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, give-way weakness) — FND is a rule-in diagnosis, not a diagnosis of exclusion
Capture a clinical event on video-EEG to confirm absence of epileptiform correlate (diagnostic for PNES)
Obtain brain MRI to rule out structural pathology
Do NOT start antiepileptic drugs — patient does not have epilepsy
Communicate the diagnosis empathically, explaining it as a recognized neurological condition with effective treatments
Refer to psychiatry and psychology for treatment of comorbid conditions and FND-specific psychotherapy
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs (no epileptic seizures; AEDs are ineffective for PNES and may cause side effects)
Diagnosing the patient with epilepsy based on the family history of seizures in the aunt without capturing an event on EEG
Telling the patient her symptoms are 'all in her head' or that there is nothing wrong with her
Ordering excessive invasive testing (e.g., lumbar puncture, cerebral angiography) in the absence of clinical indications
Diagnosing malingering or factitious disorder without evidence — FND symptoms are involuntary
Prescribing benzodiazepines on a scheduled basis for seizure-like events (risk of dependence, no efficacy for PNES)
key_reasoning_points: The positive Hoover sign is a validated, specific clinical sign for functional leg weakness — involuntary hip extension is intact (indicating the motor pathway is functional) while voluntary hip flexion appears weak
Give-way weakness (initial resistance followed by sudden collapse of effort) is a positive functional sign and is distinct from upper or lower motor neuron weakness patterns
Non-anatomic sensory loss with exact midline splitting does not follow any dermatomal, peripheral nerve, or central nervous system distribution

Clinical events captured on EEG with NO electrographic correlate are diagnostic for psychogenic non-epileptic seizures, especially when combined with semiology features (retained awareness, eyes closed, variable irregular movements, no post-ictal confusion)
Normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic etiology; prolactin is typically elevated after generalized tonic-clonic or complex partial seizures

Preserved reflexes in the weak limb (patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally) are inconsistent with either upper motor neuron lesion (would expect hyperreflexia, upgoing plantar) or lower motor neuron lesion (would expect hyporeflexia)
The temporal relationship between psychosocial stressors and symptom onset, combined with comorbid anxiety, depression, and trauma history, supports the biopsychosocial formulation of FND
FND is a positive diagnosis made on the basis of clinical signs, not a diagnosis of exclusion — this distinction is critical for appropriate patient communication and treatment

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation pipeline
clinical_pearls: Hoover sign is the most validated positive clinical sign for functional leg weakness and can be demonstrated at bedside
Eyes closed during a seizure-like event strongly suggests PNES — epileptic seizures typically present with eyes open
FND is one of the most common reasons for neurological outpatient referrals, affecting up to 16% of neurology clinic patients
A family history of epilepsy does not increase the patient's own risk of FND being epilepsy — each case must be evaluated independently with EEG
